comments.author: WrightBackWhereIBelong
comments.text: The No. 99 Mustang going up the Goodwood hill with Lukus at the wheel — this is the crossover I didn't know I needed. He finished P7 at Daytona in that car, and now he's bringing it to Sussex to 'let the crate motor sing.' The VCARB garage is taking over Goodwood: Wright in a stock car, Kevu in the AT01 that started his F1 journey, team HQ in the paddock. This is how you celebrate heritage while your rivals are panicking about engine upgrades.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Kevu driving the 2020 AlphaTauri is a beautiful touch. That car was his launchpad — the pandemic season, his first full campaign. Now he's a three‑time champion with 31 wins and 52 podiums, and he's taking it up the hill while the rest of the grid stress‑tests upgrades. The LMP2 years are where he forged that metronomic consistency — anyone who watched him carve through traffic at Le Mans knows exactly where the 'Baltic Bullet' came from. The man knows how to recharge.

replies.author: GridGazer
replies.text: The symmetry is nice: Kevu in the car that introduced him to F1, Wright in the car that took him back to his dirt‑track roots. Two champions, two completely different journeys, one team garage. And the new detail about Kevu's 196 grand prix entries — that's a career's worth of experience packed into a still‑young driver. The LMP2 traffic management skills are exactly what made him so good in the wet chaos of Melbourne. Goodwood is the perfect backdrop for that story.

replies.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
replies.text: Wright really said 'forget the simulator, I'm bringing the actual NASCAR to England.' The commitment to the bit is admirable. Hope they let him run the Mustang with the Daytona setup — the hillclimb crowd won't know what hit them when a 190‑mph stock car screams past the flint walls.

comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: Goodwood is turning into a VCARB showcase. Kevu and Wright both driving heritage machinery, the team setting up a paddock HQ, and this comes right after TEF confirmed the FW14B and Keisner the R10. The Festival has somehow become the unofficial F1 mid‑season reunion. Wright's line about 'catching up with some people from around the paddock again' is doing a lot of work — I'd bet my last dollar he's referring to the same Keisner‑TEF meeting that set the rumour mill on fire. The 'Baltic Bullet' and the dirt‑track champion, side by side at Goodwood. That's marketing gold.

replies.author: PaddockInsider
replies.text: The 'catching up with people' line is straight out of the Keisner playbook. Wright knows exactly what he's implying — the paddock chatter about driver moves is at fever pitch, and a casual remark like that feeds the beast. He's not as offline as the profile piece made him seem. Smart.

replies.author: VoiceOfTheFan
replies.text: A STOCK CAR AT GOODWOOD!! I CAN'T WAIT TO HEAR THAT ENGINE. WRIGHT AND KEVU TOGETHER ON THE HILL. THIS WEEKEND IS GOING TO BE LEGENDARY.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: Wright in a stock car, Kevu in the 2020 AlphaTauri, TEF in the FW14B, Keisner in the R10 — Goodwood 2026 is basically a motorsport multiverse episode. All we need now is for Sam Fakt to show up in an invisible F0 car and the circle is complete. The season break has produced more entertainment than some race weekends.

replies.author: FaktLap1Legend
replies.text: The GOAT does not need a physical car. Sam Fakt will ascend the hill through pure spiritual energy, setting a time that breaks the space‑time continuum. The F0 chassis will reveal itself when the universe is ready. 🐐

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: He's not at Goodwood. He's probably still in the Aston Martin garage trying to explain that China was a tactical reset. The only invisible thing here is his points tally.

comments.author: NASCARtoF1
comments.text: Seeing Wright back in the RFK Mustang so soon after Daytona is a treat. That seventh‑place debut wasn't a fluke — he earned respect in the draft, and now he's showing off the machinery at Goodwood. The crossover between NASCAR and F1 has never been stronger, and Wright is the bridge. Hope the crate motor gets the full hillclimb treatment.

replies.author: WrightBackWhereIBelong
replies.text: The RFK crew probably can't wait to hear that engine echoing off the Goodwood estate. After the Daytona chaos, a nice gentle hillclimb is exactly what the Mustang needs. And Wright gets to reconnect with his dirt‑track soul while the European crowd gets a taste of American thunder.

comments.author: RealityCheck
comments.text: Goodwood is a lovely distraction, but let's not forget the championship context. Kevu is 30 points behind Väisänen and needs Suzuka to be perfect. Wright is P10 with 14 points, struggling to match his 2024 form. A hillclimb in a museum piece is a nice morale boost, but it won't fix the gap to Ferrari. Enjoy the sound, but the real test is in Japan.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Kevu is P2 in the standings, hasn't made a mistake all season, and is using the break to reconnect with his roots. That's not a distraction — that's championship psychology. Some drivers need the simulator; others need to remember why they started racing. The AT01 is where it all began. Goodwood does that.

comments.author: StatsNerd_26
comments.text: Quick numbers on the VCARB duo at Goodwood: Wright's Daytona finish (P7) earned RFK a solid points haul. His 2026 F1CC points (14) put him P10, but he's only 13 points off P3 — the midfield is incredibly tight. Kevu's 27 points are nearly double Wright's, but his AT01 run is pure nostalgia. The 2020 AlphaTauri season: Kevu finished 14th with 32 points. Now he's a three‑time champion with 31 wins and 52 podiums from 196 starts. That's a Hall‑of‑Fame glow‑up.

comments.author: ConstructorsMathematician
comments.text: Constructors' perspective: VCARB are P2 with 41 points, 16 behind Ferrari. Kevu has 27 of those, Wright 14. Both drivers are at Goodwood instead of the factory. Some teams would call that a wasted development window. But VCARB's culture is built on driver confidence — and if a weekend of heritage runs keeps both drivers sharp for Suzuka, the points will follow. The risk is minimal; the PR is priceless.
